title: How Tardigrades Survive the Vacuum of Space
summary: Tardigrades survive space-like extremes by entering cryptobiosis, losing water, forming a tun, slowing metabolism, stabilizing cells, and repairing molecular damage after rehydration.

lesson.learn: Tardigrades survive conditions that would kill most animals because they can temporarily stop living in the usual active sense. When water disappears, many species enter cryptobiosis. They contract into a compact tun, lose most body water, and slow metabolism to a near standstill. In that state, cellular structures are stabilized by protective proteins, antioxidant systems, and DNA-repair mechanisms. The vacuum of space is dangerous because it removes pressure, strips water, and exposes organisms to radiation and extreme temperature swings. Tardigrades can survive vacuum exposure far better than ordinary animals when dried and dormant, though radiation and ultraviolet light still reduce survival. Their trick is not thriving in space; it is pausing damage long enough to restart.
lesson.simpler: Tardigrades survive space vacuum by drying into a dormant tun state that protects cells until water and safer conditions return.
lesson.keyTakeaway: Tardigrade space survival depends on cryptobiosis, a reversible shutdown that stabilizes cells during dehydration and vacuum exposure, then relies on repair when conditions improve.
lesson.deeperDive: The famous space experiments did not show tardigrades casually crawling around in open space. They showed that some dehydrated tardigrades could survive exposure to vacuum and cosmic radiation, especially when shielded from the harshest ultraviolet radiation. Cryptobiosis solves several problems at once. With little water present, ice crystals cannot form easily, chemical reactions slow, and the organism becomes less vulnerable to immediate metabolic collapse. Tardigrade-specific intrinsically disordered proteins may stabilize cell contents, while DNA-repair systems help after rehydration. Different species vary in tolerance, and active hydrated tardigrades are much more fragile than dried tuns.
lesson.funFact: A tardigrade in cryptobiosis is not invincible, but it can wait out conditions that would destroy most animals.
references: Current Biology - TARDIS tardigrades in space experiment
Nature Communications - tardigrade damage suppression proteins

people: Ingemar Jonsson - researcher associated with tardigrade space-exposure experiments
Takekazu Kunieda - researcher on tardigrade stress-protection proteins
J. A. E. Goeze - early describer of water bears in the 18th century
Astrobiologists - scientists who study life under space-like extremes
places: Low Earth orbit - environment used for tardigrade exposure experiments
Tun state - contracted dormant body form
Moss and lichen habitats - common places to find terrestrial tardigrades
Laboratory rehydration chambers - settings where dormant tardigrades are revived
years: 2007 - tardigrades are exposed to space in the TARDIS experiment
2016 - studies identify tardigrade proteins involved in stress tolerance
Present day - tardigrades inform astrobiology and preservation research
concepts: Cryptobiosis - reversible dormant state under extreme stress
DNA repair - correction of molecular damage
Vitrification - glass-like stabilization of cell contents
